cards.text: {{c1::Stack unwinding}} is the process of removing function calls from the call stack when an exception is thrown, until a matching {{c2::exception handler}} is found.

cards.front: What happens when a function throws an exception that it doesn't catch?
cards.back: The exception propagates up the call stack to the calling function. This continues until a matching catch block is found or the program terminates.

cards.question: Where is the exception caught?
cards.explanation: The exception thrown in C() propagates through B() (which has no handler) up to A() where it's caught.

cards.text: During stack unwinding, all local objects in the scope being exited are {{c1::destroyed}} (their destructors are called). This ensures proper {{c2::resource cleanup}} following RAII principles.

cards.question: Is the file properly closed if processFile() throws?
cards.answer: Yes, the file is properly closed.
cards.explanation: Even if processFile() throws, stack unwinding will destroy the file object, calling its destructor which closes the file. This is RAII in action.

cards.front: What happens if an exception is thrown from main() and not caught?
cards.back: std::terminate() is called, which typically calls std::abort() and the program terminates abnormally without proper cleanup.

cards.text: When searching for an exception handler, only the {{c1::type}} of the exception matters, not its {{c2::value}}. The search proceeds up the {{c3::call stack}} function by function.

cards.question: Will this code catch the exception?
cards.answer: No, the exception won't be caught and std::terminate() will be called.
cards.explanation: The catch block expects an int, but a std::runtime_error is thrown. Type mismatch means the exception isn't caught.

cards.concept: Why RAII is critical for exception safety
cards.explanation:
Stack unwinding during exceptions automatically calls destructors of local objects. RAII ensures resources are freed:
  • File handles are closed
  • Memory is deallocated
  • Locks are released
  • Network connections are closed
Without RAII, exceptions would leak resources.

cards.front: Can a try block only catch exceptions from directly called functions, or also from nested function calls?
cards.back: A try block catches exceptions from any function in the call chain below it, not just directly called functions. Exceptions propagate up through all levels.

cards.text: If no exception handler is found for a thrown exception, {{c1::std::terminate()}} is called, which by default calls {{c2::std::abort()}} to abnormally terminate the program.

cards.question: How many functions are unwound from the stack?
cards.answer: Three functions: d(), c(), and b() are unwound before the exception is caught in a().
cards.explanation: Stack unwinding removes all functions from the call stack between the throw point and the catch handler.
